citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two adult female goats (one black horned La Mancha and one dark fawn La Mancha) had severely overgrown hooves.
Both showed abnormal spreading of the digits on the front feet due to excessive hoof growth. The rear claws were
elongated and slipper like; on the black La Mancha, the rear claws were also crossing over.
‑
Overgrown hooves can cause pain, discomfort, lameness, and increase the risk of infection or injury.
All exhibitors must maintain a program of veterinary care that includes appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnosis
and treat injury and disease including corrective and routine preventive hoof care.
Correct by 5/15/2026.

The rabbit (7) feeders were crusted and dirty with leftover feed, feces and debris. The feeders were rusted and pitted such
that they would not be able to be sanitized effectively.
Unclean and unsanitized feeders could contaminate the feed leading to reduced intake, illness, distress, malnutrition or
death of the animals.
All food receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitized at least once every two weeks.
To be corrected by 5/5/2026.

There were significant accumulations of feces and hair packed into piles in the corners and mesh flooring of the rabbit (7)
cages. These piles were extremely dry and mounded. Excessive fecal pellets were also present on the ground beneath
the cages and had not been removed.
Such buildup of fecal and waste material can cause distress, illness, disease, death of the animals, and attract pests.
Primary enclosures shall be kept reasonably free of fecal material, hair and other debris by periodic cleaning and shall be
sanitized at least once every 30 days. Fecal material should be removed from underneath enclosures with wire or mesh
floors at least once each week.

Multiple areas of the facility were in disrepair, creating sharp hazards and structural risks. In the red fox (2) enclosure,
several dug out areas exposed the no dig chain link barrier, and sharp wires were protruding into the animals’ space at
‑ ‑ ‑
their level. In the camel (2) paddock, fence rails had come loose from the posts, creating a large gap. In the male goat (7)
pen, a sheet metal panel approximately 1 ft by 5 ft had detached from the shed wall and was hanging and flapping within
‑
reach of the goats.
Sharp points, edges, and unstable materials pose a risk of injury, illness, or death to the animals. Poorly maintained
fencing and housing also increase the likelihood of animal escape or entrapment.
All indoor and outdoor housing facilities should be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals
from injury and to contain the animals.

Several water tubs and buckets in pens containing the 4 Kune-kune pigs, 3 raccoons, and 2 foxes had large amounts of
debris, slime, and sludge accumulating on the bottom of the water containers and the water was cloudy and dark with
floating debris.
Dirty water tubs can contaminate the water leading to reduced water intake, illness or death.
All water receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two adult female goats (one black horned La Mancha and one dark fawn La Mancha) had severely overgrown hooves. 
Both showed abnormal spreading of the digits on the front feet due to excessive hoof growth. The rear claws were 
elongated and slipper‑like; on the black La Mancha, the rear claws were also crossing over. 
Overgrown hooves can cause pain, discomfort, lameness, and increase the risk of infection or injury. 
All exhibitors must maintain a program of veterinary care that includes appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnosis 
and treat injury and disease including corrective and routine preventive hoof care. 
Correct by 5/15/2026. 
3.54(b) 
   
Feeding. 
The rabbit (7) feeders were crusted and dirty with leftover feed, feces and debris. The feeders were rusted and pitted such 
that they would not be able to be sanitized effectively. 
Unclean and unsanitized feeders could contaminate the feed leading to reduced intake, illness, distress, malnutrition or 
death of the animals. 
All food receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitized at least once every two weeks. 
To be corrected by 5/5/2026. 
3.56(a)  Repeat
   
Sanitation. 
There were significant accumulations of feces and hair packed into piles in the corners and mesh flooring of the rabbit (7) 
cages. These piles were extremely dry and mounded. Excessive fecal pellets were also present on the ground beneath 
the cages and had not been removed. 
Such buildup of fecal and waste material can cause distress, illness, disease, death of the animals, and attract pests. 
Primary enclosures shall be kept reasonably free of fecal material, hair and other debris by periodic cleaning and shall be 
sanitized at least once every 30 days. Fecal material should be removed from underneath enclosures with wire or mesh 
floors at least once each week. 
3.125(a)  Repeat

Facilities, general. 
Multiple areas of the facility were in disrepair, creating sharp hazards and structural risks. In the red fox (2) enclosure, 
several dug‑out areas exposed the no‑dig chain‑link barrier, and sharp wires were protruding into the animals’ space at 
their level. In the camel (2) paddock, fence rails had come loose from the posts, creating a large gap. In the male goat (7) 
pen, a sheet‑metal panel approximately 1 ft by 5 ft had detached from the shed wall and was hanging and flapping within 
reach of the goats.  
 
Sharp points, edges, and unstable materials pose a risk of injury, illness, or death to the animals. Poorly maintained 
fencing and housing also increase the likelihood of animal escape or entrapment. 
 
All indoor and outdoor housing facilities should be structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals 
from injury and to contain the animals. 
3.130  Repeat
   
Watering. 
Several water tubs and buckets in pens containing the 4 Kune-kune pigs, 3 raccoons, and 2 foxes had large amounts of 
debris, slime, and sludge accumulating on the bottom of the water containers and the water was cloudy and dark with 
floating debris. 
Dirty water tubs can contaminate the water leading to reduced water intake, illness or death. 
All water receptacles shall be kept clean and sanitary. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
